prompt_text: How do I manage customer relationships without spending a lot on software?
response:
You can manage customer relationships very effectively with **simple processes plus free/low-cost tools**—you do not need an expensive CRM suite to get started.

Here’s a practical approach you can implement in stages, keeping costs near zero:

---

## 1. Get your basics right before any software

Even with no tools beyond email and a spreadsheet, you can improve relationships by standardizing how you work:

- **Define what “a good relationship” means for you**
  - Examples: quick response times, regular check-ins, remembering key details (preferences, history), proactive follow-ups.
  - Pick 3–5 measurable goals such as:
    - Respond to all customer emails within 24 hours.
    - Follow up with every lead at least 3 times unless they say no.
    - Check in with top customers quarterly.

- **Standardize your workflow**
  - Write down, step-by-step:
    - How a new lead comes in.
    - What happens in the first 7 days (emails/calls).
    - When and how you follow up if they don’t respond.
    - How you onboard a new customer.
    - How you keep in touch after the sale.

- **Decide what information you always capture**
  - At minimum:
    - Name, company, role
    - Email, phone, preferred channel (email/WhatsApp/phone)
    - How they found you
    - What they’re interested in / main problem
    - Status (lead, active customer, past customer, lost)
    - Last contact date and next follow-up date

This “manual CRM strategy” mirrors what larger systems do, just in a lightweight way.[6][8]

---

## 2. Start with free tools you probably already have

If your budget is near zero, combine **spreadsheets + email + calendar**:

- **Spreadsheet as a simple CRM**
  - Use Google Sheets, Excel Online, or similar.
  - Create columns for:
    - Customer name
    - Company
    - Email / phone
    - Source (referral, website, event, etc.)
    - Status (new lead, in discussion, proposal sent, won, lost)
    - Last contact date
    - Next action / follow-up date
    - Notes
  - Filter by “Next action date” so you know who to contact each day.

- **Calendar reminders**
  - When you add “Next action date” for a contact, also add a calendar event:
    - Title: “Follow up – [Customer Name]”
    - Description: copy key notes and your next step.
  - This substitutes for the reminder/automation in a CRM.

- **Email templates**
  - Save 3–5 standard templates in your email client:
    - Initial response to a new inquiry
    - First follow-up if no reply
    - Second follow-up
    - “Nice to meet you” after a call/meeting
    - Check-in with existing customers
  - This speeds you up and creates consistent communication.

This setup is essentially free and can work surprisingly well up to dozens or even a few hundred relationships.

---

## 3. Upgrade to a free CRM when manual tracking gets painful

Once your spreadsheet is hard to maintain or you’re missing follow-ups, use a **free CRM** instead of paying for something complex.

Well-known free options include:

- **HubSpot CRM (free)**  
  - Offers contact management, activity history, deal pipeline, email tracking, tasks, and meeting scheduling in a single place.[5]  
  - Free with no time limit; works in the browser.[5]  
  - Good if you want a more polished, “all-in-one” feel.

- Other free CRMs commonly recommended for small businesses:
  - Many lists of **free or low-cost CRM tools** highlight options like HubSpot, Freshsales, and others with free tiers suitable for small teams.[1][3][7]

Most free plans typically give you:
- **Contact database & management** with customizable fields.[1][3]
- **Simple sales pipeline** to track leads by stage.[1][3]
- **Basic email integration** and activity logging.[1][5]
- **Task/follow-up reminders**.[1][5]

You can run your whole relationship process on these free plans before you ever pay for software.

---

## 4. Keep your data and setup very simple

To avoid getting overwhelmed (and to stay on free tiers), keep your CRM usage lean:

- **Create only a few pipeline stages**, for example:
  - New lead
  - Contacted
  - Qualified / interested
  - Proposal / quote sent
  - Won
  - Lost

- **Limit custom fields** to what you actually use:
  - Industry, deal size, product of interest, key decision maker.

- **Use tasks and reminders religiously**
  - Every time you finish a call or
